other_observers: Hannah Floyd and I discovered the bird and saw it together. The bird was subsequently observed by many others, including Tim Smart, Daniel Maynard, Peter Gent, Christian Nunes, and Julia Bond.; Yes, with Hannah Floyd.
specific_location: I first observed the bird from 39.991128 N, 105.212637 W. Then I observed the bird from 39.994663 N, 105.202900 W.
description: I saw the bird on open water on Baseline Reservoir. It was notably smaller than nearby Ring-billed Gulls. At times it spun around in circles, like a phalarope. The bird was pale overall, being white below and grayish above. Its back and wings ("mantle" on the swimming bird) were fairly uniformly colored, i.e., they did not show any obvious patterning. The bird's mainly white head showed a thick black post-ocular patch (the "phalarope patch") and gray-black smudging on the crown. The bill was dark and notably thick, with some paler (yellow or "flesh") coloration toward the base. I'm not sure of the bird's plumage. In essence, it was in "winter plumage," but I don't know if it was a bird coming out of juvenal plumage or an adult in mostly basic plumage. In any event, it was neither in (mainly) juvenal plumage nor in alternate/adult plumage.Behaviors: As noted above, I observed the bird swimming out on the middle of Baseline Reservoir. On several occasions, the bird was spinning around like a phalarope. After the initial period of observation, I moved to another vantage point along the lakeshore, and I observed the bird swimming unidirectionally for at least 100 meters.Call: None heard.Plumage: see below
